title: Brain criticality through nonadditive entropic analysis of electroencephalograms
authors: Henrique Santos Lima, Constantino Tsallis, Dimitri M. Abramov
abstract: On the grounds of nonadditive entropies -- appropriate for complex systems -- we investigate the electroencephalogram amplitudes of typical and ADHD children. The corresponding probability distributions are $q$-Gaussians, i.e., $ρ(x) \propto e_q^{-βx^2} \equiv [1+(q-1) βx^2]^{1/(1-q)}$, where $(q,β)$ are, respectively, the entropic index characterizing complexity and the inverse width. We show that $q$ tends to monotonically vary with $β$ for both typical and ADHD subjects, thus revealing critical behavior of the brain. Moreover, we verify that ADHD subjects have a higher complexity than the typical ones. Consistently, biomarkers for objective phychyatric diagnosis could emerge along this path. We show that $q$ tends to monotonically vary with $β$ for both typical and ADHD subjects, thus revealing critical behavior of the brain. Moreover, we verify that ADHD subjects have a higher complexity than the typical ones. Consistently, biomarkers for objective phychyatric diagnosis could emerge along this path.
summary: This paper applies nonadditive statistical mechanics to analyze EEG data from children. It demonstrates that brain activity follows q-Gaussian distributions and that ADHD is characterized by higher levels of complexity, potentially providing a path toward objective diagnostic tools.
main_problem: Investigating the complexity and critical behavior of brain activity in typical versus ADHD children using nonadditive entropic frameworks.
main_result: The study finds that EEG amplitudes follow q-Gaussian distributions and that ADHD subjects exhibit higher complexity than typical subjects, suggesting potential biomarkers for psychiatric diagnosis.
method: Nonadditive entropic analysis of electroencephalogram (EEG) amplitudes using q-Gaussian probability distributions.
model_or_system: The system consists of brain activity as measured by EEG amplitudes in typical and ADHD children, modeled through nonadditive statistics.
key_observables: EEG amplitudes, entropic index (q), and inverse width (beta).
important_parameters: The entropic index q, which characterizes complexity, and the parameter beta.
main_assumptions: The brain activity can be appropriately described by nonadditive entropies and q-Gaussian distributions.
paper_structure: The paper presents an investigation of EEG amplitudes using nonadditive entropy, compares typical and ADHD subjects, and discusses the implications for psychiatric biomarkers.
